content.value: Quickly recap everything we've learned in this section.

items: 1.1 Docker Basics: Introduction to Docker as a platform to build, ship, and run apps.
1.2 Docker Architecture: Client-Server model consisting of Docker Client, Host (Daemon), and Registry.
1.3 Containerization: Lightweight virtualization that bundles app code with dependencies.
1.4 Containers vs VMs: Containers share the host kernel (fast, light) while VMs virtualize hardware (heavy, secure).
1.5 Docker Evolution: History from dotCloud to modern modular architecture (containerd, runc).
1.6 Docker Developments: Ecosystem tools like Docker Desktop, Engine, and Hub.
1.7 Docker Tooling: Advanced tools like Docker Machine, Compose, and Swarm.
1.8 Basic Commands: Essential CLI commands for managing images and container lifecycles.

content.value: Test your knowledge with these interactive questions.

content.title: 1. What is the primary difference between a Docker Image and a Container?

contentBlocks.value: An **Image** is a read-only template (a blueprint), while a **Container** is a running instance of that image. You can have many containers running from a single image.

content.title: 2. Which Linux kernel features make containerization possible?

contentBlocks.value: Containerization relies on **Namespaces** (for isolation) and **Cgroups** (for resource limiting).

content.title: 3. What is the role of the Docker Daemon?

contentBlocks.value: The **Docker Daemon (dockerd)** listens for Docker API requests and manages Docker objects such as images, containers, networks, and volumes.

content.value: These questions are frequently asked in VTU theory exams and interviews.

content.title: Q1. Compare and Contrast Virtual Machines and Containers. (8 Marks)

contentBlocks.value: Focus on: Hypervisor vs Docker Engine, Guest OS vs Shared Kernel, Boot time, Resource efficiency, and Isolation levels.

content.title: Q2. Explain the Docker Client-Server Architecture with a neat diagram. (10 Marks)

contentBlocks.value: Explain the interaction between the Client, the Docker Host (Daemon), and the Registry. Mention commands like build, pull, and run.

content.title: Q3. Discuss the Docker Container Life Cycle with suitable commands. (8 Marks)

contentBlocks.value: Describe states: Created, Running, Paused, Stopped, and Deleted. Link them to commands: create, start, run, pause, stop, and rm.

content.value: Congratulations! You have completed the first section of the Docker Masterclass. Ready to dive into Networking?
